g.data-narration: Binary tree representation covers linked and array methods for storing tree data structures
A binary tree is hierarchical with at most two children per node: left and right
g.text: node has at most 2 children: left & right
Used in: BSTs, Heaps, Expression Trees

g.data-narration: Choosing the right representation impacts memory, speed, and implementation complexity
Linked representation uses structs with data and left right pointer fields
Example shows nodes A through E with their memory links stored as pointers
Linked representation offers dynamic sizing but uses extra memory for pointers
Array representation works best for complete trees using direct index formulas
g.text: Complete Binary Trees → Direct Array Indexing
Incomplete Trees → Modified Array with Placeholders

g.data-narration: Key formulas: parent at floor of i-1/2, left child at 2i+1, right child at 2i+2
Complete binary tree with indices shown maps directly to array positions
Applying formulas: left child of index 1 is 2×1+1=3, right child is 2×1+2=4
Incomplete trees use placeholder values like -1 for missing nodes
Linked uses O(n) plus pointer overhead; array uses O(2^h) space
g.text: Linked: O(n) + pointer overhead (4/8 bytes per pointer)
Array: O(2^h) where h = height (wasteful for incomplete)

g.data-narration: Linked construction shows building tree with malloc for each node
Complete tree example showing level order mapping to array indices
Incomplete tree uses placeholder -1 for missing positions in array
Choose linked for dynamic trees, array for heaps and complete trees
g.text: Array: Complete trees, heaps, priority queues

g.data-narration: Remember parent uses floor of i-1 over 2, left child is 2i+1, right child is 2i+2
